steps.title: Sketch the building wall (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch the building wall as a flat rectangle. The fire escape will sit in front of it — having the wall plane established first gives you the perspective context and a surface to anchor the structure against.

steps.title: Sketch the platform landings (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch the main platform at each level as a rectangular grid viewed in perspective — these are the landing surfaces. Each platform sits slightly below its corresponding floor's window. The horizontal edges follow the building's perspective; the vertical posts are truly vertical.

steps.title: Add the vertical corner posts (pencil)
steps.description: Place four vertical lines at the corners of each platform — these are the structural posts. Vertical lines are plumb in this view, so check each one against the true vertical of the building corner.

steps.title: Sketch the stair runs between levels (pencil)
steps.description: Between platforms, sketch the diagonal stair runs — each is a narrow parallelogram in perspective, angling downward from upper platform to lower platform. The railing on each run parallels the stair's angle.

steps.title: Add the railing posts (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch short vertical lines spaced evenly along the platform edges and stair railings. These small repeated verticals give the structure its visible rhythm.

steps.title: Sketch the drop ladder at the bottom (pencil)
steps.description: The lowest landing usually has a retractable ladder rather than a fixed stair. Sketch it as a simple rectangle with short horizontal rungs crossing it — like a very narrow ladder.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the platforms
steps.description: Switch to ink. Ink each platform as a clean geometric grid — horizontal edges follow the building perspective, vertical posts are truly straight. These flat surfaces are the most legible part of the structure.

steps.title: Ink the stairs, railings, and ladder (ink)
steps.description: Ink the stair runs and their railings, then the vertical railing posts. Use slightly heavier lines on the edges nearest to the viewer — the near rail of each platform and the front-facing stair handrail. That weight difference reads as depth without any shading.
